name: commit
context: Stage files and create git commits with well-structured messages following conventional formats
info: The commit skill covers the full staging-and-commit workflow: selectively staging files with git add, unstaging with git reset, crafting commit messages following conventional commit format, amending the last commit, creating empty commits, and using git commit with various flags. Agents use this constantly — every code change must be committed. The staging area (index) acts as a buffer between the working tree and the repository, letting you compose commits precisely. Good commit messages are critical for history readability; this skill teaches the conventional commits format (type(scope): description) and when to use each type. See [[git/diff]] to review changes before committing, [[git/log]] to verify commit history, and [[git/stash]] to shelve work before committing something else.
triggers: how to write good commit messages
body:
# Git Commit

Committing is the most fundamental git operation. Every meaningful change to a codebase becomes a commit — a permanent snapshot with a message explaining why.

## Staging Files

The staging area (index) lets you compose exactly what goes into a commit.

```bash
# Stage specific files
git add src/auth.js test/auth.test.js

# Stage all changes in a directory
git add src/

# Stage all tracked file changes (not new files)
git add -u

# Stage everything (use with caution)
git add -A

# Stage hunks interactively (for splitting changes)
git add -p
```

### Unstaging

```bash
# Unstage a file (keep working tree changes)
git reset HEAD src/auth.js

# Unstage everything
git reset HEAD

# Modern alternative (git 2.23+)
git restore --staged src/auth.js
```

## Creating Commits

```bash
# Commit staged changes with inline message
git commit -m "feat(auth): add JWT token validation"

# Commit with multi-line message
git commit -m "fix(api): handle null response from upstream" -m "The payment gateway occasionally returns null instead of an error object. Added null check before accessing response.status."

# Open editor for message
git commit

# Stage all tracked changes and commit in one step
git commit -am "chore: update dependencies"

# Create an empty commit (useful for triggering CI)
git commit --allow-empty -m "ci: trigger rebuild"
```

## Conventional Commits Format

The standard format for commit messages in professional codebases:

```
<type>(<scope>): <description>

[optional body]

[optional footer(s)]
```

### Types

| Type | When to use |
|------|-------------|
| `feat` | New feature or capability |
| `fix` | Bug fix |
| `docs` | Documentation only |
| `style` | Formatting, whitespace (no logic change) |
| `refactor` | Code change that neither fixes nor adds |
| `perf` | Performance improvement |
| `test` | Adding or correcting tests |
| `chore` | Build, CI, tooling, dependencies |
| `ci` | CI/CD configuration changes |
| `revert` | Reverting a previous commit |

### Examples

```
feat(auth): add OAuth2 provider support

Added Google and GitHub OAuth2 providers to the authentication
module. Users can now sign in with their existing accounts.

Closes #142
```

```
fix(api): prevent race condition in concurrent requests

Mutex was not held during the read-modify-write cycle in the
session store. Added proper locking around the critical section.
```

## Amending Commits

```bash
# Amend the last commit message
git commit --amend -m "fix(api): correct error handling"

# Amend to add forgotten files (keep same message)
git add forgotten-file.js
git commit --amend --no-edit
```

**Warning**: Never amend commits that have been pushed to a shared branch. This rewrites history and forces others to re-sync. See [[git/rebase]] for rewriting older commits.

## Reviewing Before Committing

Always review what you're about to commit:

```bash
# See what's staged (will be committed)
git diff --staged

# See what's changed but not staged
git diff

# See status overview
git status

# See specific file changes
git diff --staged -- src/auth.js
```

See [[git/diff]] for comprehensive diff usage.

## Signing Commits

```bash
# Sign with GPG
git commit -S -m "feat: signed commit"

# Always sign (configure globally)
git config --global commit.gpgsign true
```

## Common Patterns for Agents

1. **Always stage specific files** — avoid `git add -A` which can catch secrets or build artifacts
2. **Check status before committing** — `git status` prevents surprises
3. **Review diffs before committing** — `git diff --staged` ensures you commit what you intend
4. **Write descriptive messages** — future agents and humans read these
5. **One logical change per commit** — don't mix refactoring with features

sections.title: Staging files with git add
sections.body: Use `git add <path>` to stage specific files or directories. Use `git add -p` to stage individual hunks for splitting changes across commits. Use `git add -u` to stage modifications to tracked files without adding new untracked files. Avoid `git add -A` in automated contexts — it can accidentally stage secrets, `.env` files, or build artifacts. Always verify with `git status` after staging. Unstage with `git restore --staged <path>` (git 2.23+) or `git reset HEAD <path>`.

sections.title: Commit message conventions
sections.body: Use conventional commits format: `type(scope): description`. The type classifies the change (feat, fix, docs, refactor, test, chore, perf, ci, style, revert). The scope is optional and identifies the module affected. The description is imperative mood, lowercase, no period. The body (after blank line) explains why, not what. Footers reference issues (`Closes #42`) or note breaking changes (`BREAKING CHANGE: removed v1 API`). Good messages make [[git/log]] and `git blame` useful.

sections.title: Amending the last commit
sections.body: Use `git commit --amend` to modify the most recent commit. Add `--no-edit` to keep the existing message while adding staged changes. Add `-m "new message"` to change the message. Amending rewrites the commit hash, so never amend commits already pushed to shared branches. For rewriting older history, see [[git/rebase]]. Amending is safe for local-only commits and is commonly used to fix typos or add forgotten files.

sections.title: Signing commits with GPG or SSH
sections.body: Sign commits with `git commit -S` using GPG, or configure SSH signing with `git config gpg.format ssh` and `git config user.signingkey <key>`. Enable auto-signing with `git config commit.gpgsign true`. Signed commits show a 'Verified' badge on GitHub. See [[git/config]] for global signing configuration.

Commits advance the current branch pointer

scripts.name: Commit Staged Changes
scripts.description: Stage specific files and create a conventional commit
scripts.inline:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Usage: commit-staged.sh <type> <scope> <message> [files...]
# Example: commit-staged.sh feat auth "add JWT validation" src/auth.js test/auth.test.js

TYPE="${1:?Usage: commit-staged.sh <type> <scope> <message> [files...]}"
SCOPE="${2:?Missing scope}"
MSG="${3:?Missing message}"
shift 3

if [ $# -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "Error: No files specified. Stage files explicitly."
  exit 1
fi

# Stage specified files
for f in "$@"; do
  if [ ! -e "$f" ]; then
    echo "Warning: $f does not exist, skipping"
    continue
  fi
  git add "$f"
done

# Verify something is staged
if git diff --cached --quiet; then
  echo "Nothing staged to commit."
  exit 1
fi

# Show what will be committed
echo "=== Staged changes ==="
git diff --cached --stat
echo

# Commit
if [ -n "$SCOPE" ] && [ "$SCOPE" != "-" ]; then
  git commit -m "${TYPE}(${SCOPE}): ${MSG}"
else
  git commit -m "${TYPE}: ${MSG}"
fi

echo
git log --oneline -1

amend.description: Amend the last commit instead of creating new

sha.description: Full SHA of the created commit

depends.reason: Author identity must be configured before committing
